content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 8, 2020

Amber Mace
Executive Director
California Council on Science and Technology
1130 K St, Suite 280
Sacramento, CA 95814-3965

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-19-238

Dear Ms. Mace:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift, bequested payment and contribution provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act, and not under gift or similar prohibitions outside the Act.

Also, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Where the California Council on Science and Technology ("CCST") is a 501(c)(3) organization that employs Science Fellows placed in the state Executive branch, does the payment of the Fellow's salaries result in a gift, bequested payment or contribution under the Act?

2. Where a 501(c)(3) organization has specifically earmarked funds for a CCST Fellow to be placed at a particular agency for work on a specific issue-type, is the donor to CCST considered the reportable source of the payments?

CONCLUSIONS

1. CCST's payments to its Science Fellows placed within the state Executive branch are neither gifts nor contributions under the Act. However, these are "bequested payments," and the elected officer or member of the Public Utilities Commission ("PUC") who coordinates or consents

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-238
Page No. 2

to the placement must file a behested payment report within 30 days of the payment equaling
$5,000 in a calendar year pursuant to Section 84224. The payments are also reportable income to
the Fellow and may create a prohibited financial conflict of interest as to government decisions in
which the Fellow participates.

2. Yes. Where behested payment reports are applicable, the report should reflect any single
source donor whose behested payments equal or exceed $5,000 in a calendar year. Please note that
payments for a Fellow placed at an agency that does not involve an elected officer or PUC member,
or agent thereof, will not meet that definition of a behested payment, and no behested payment
report is required.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

California Science and Technology Policy Fellows Program

The California Council on Science and Technology ("CCST"), an independent 501(c)(3)
nonprofit, was created at the request of the Legislature to provide California with objective,
nonpartisan advice on policy issues that involve science and technology. CCST currently
administers the California Science and Technology Policy Fellows Program ("CCST Science
Fellows") run through the Legislature. Since its launch in 2009, CCST Science Fellows are
embedded in Assembly and Senate committee offices with the goals of the CCST Science Fellows
learning the realities of translating science and technology concerns into a policy context and
providing science savvy staff to decision makers addressing complex scientific and technical issues.
Fellows research issues, assess policies, and draft bills based on facts and the latest science.
Importantly, the CCST Science Fellows work on a broad range of issues that most often have no
relation whatsoever to their PhD research. To date, CCST Science Fellows have worked on over
1,000 bills and over 50 percent of the CCST Science Fellows are hired into permanent positions in
the Legislature or state agencies to continue bringing science to policy. The fellowship program is
administered and managed by CCST and guided by the CCST Board of Directors.

In 2009 the Legislature enacted Section 8924.5 to authorize the Fellows Program and to
provide a narrow exception to Section 8920, which prohibits a member of the Legislature or an
employee from either house of the Legislature from receiving or agreeing to receive, directly or
indirectly, any compensation, reward, or gift from any source except the State of California for any
service, advice, assistance, or other matter related to the legislative process, except for specified
circumstances.

Expansion of Fellowship Program to Executive Branch

In 2019, CCST received state funding to continue the program in the Legislative branch and
allow for the expansion of the program into the Executive branch. In future years you anticipate
funding will come from private gifts or from state agencies. The funding will be given to CCST,
and it is CCST that will pay for the Fellows' salaries and expenses. You wish to know how CCST's
payment of Fellowship costs, where it involves non-state funds, is treated under the Act.

File No. I-19-238
Page No. 3

Specific Earmarked Funding for a Fellow Placement

In some instances, a specific donor earmarks funds to be used by CCST in the event it
places a Fellow with a particular agency. You provided the example that Bechtold Foundation, a
501(c)(3), has earmarked funds to be used by CCST in the event it places a Fellow with the natural
Resources Agency, for the purpose of working on "water issues." You wish to know if the donor of
the earmarked funds is the source of the Fellow payments for purposes of any reporting
requirements under the Act.

ANALYSIS

Question 1

Gifts, Contributions and Behested Payments

CCST funds the Fellowship Program and pays the salary of its employees placed as Fellows
in the Executive branch. CCST currently receives state funds to pay for the Fellows. In the future
CCST will pay for its Fellows' salaries with donated funds, and possibly with some state agency
funds. We examine how CCST's payments are treated under the Act.

The Act regulates gifts, contributions and behested payments in regard to public officials.³
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts
by public officials in three ways. First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by
certain public officials. The current limit is $500 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section
89503; Regulation 18940.2.) Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences
from gifts, the Act imposes reporting obligations on designated employees requiring that any gift
(or any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year
are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 82030 and 87302.) Third,
the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her
position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts
with an aggregate value of $500 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within
the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(c); Regulations
18700 and 18941.)

A "gift" is defined under the Act as any payment that confers a personal benefit on the
recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate is made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to official status. (Section 82028.)

A "behested payment" is defined as a payment that is made at the behest of a committee, an
elected officer, a member of the Public Utilities Commission, or an agent thereof, under particular
circumstances that distinguish the payment from a contribution (a payment for political purposes) or
a gift. (Section 82004.5.) So that the public is made aware of potential influences from behested

_______________
³ We do not have jurisdiction over Government Code Sections 8920 or 8924.5, discussed above, and we note
Section 8924.5's provisions are applicable only to the members of the Legislature in regard to the CCST Fellowships.

File No. 1-19-238
Page No. 4

payments, the Act imposes reporting obligations on elected officers and members of the PUC.
(Section 8224.)

Pertinent to these facts, a behested payment is one where it is clear from the "surrounding
circumstances that the payment was made for purposes unrelated to the officer's seeking or holding
of elective office." Payments by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organizations are presumed to be for
purposes other than an officer seeking or holding office, as are payments made for a legislative or
governmental purpose by a person other than a governmental agency. (Section 82004.5(c)(3) and
(4).)

Where a payment is "made at the behest of" an elected officer or member of the PUC, the
behesting officer must file a behested payment disclosure for payments that equal or exceed $5,000
from the same source in a calendar year. (Section 84224.) "Made at the behest of" is defined as
payments made "under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of" a candidate,
committee, elected officer, member of the PUC, or any agent thereof. (Section 82041.3 and
Regulation 18225.7.)

The Fellows placement in the Executive branch as described would not confer a "personal
benefit" to a recipient, and therefore does not meet the definition of a "gift" to an official under the
Act. This is consistent with our previous advice to CCST regarding the placement of its Fellows in
the Legislative branch. (Garamendi Advice Letter, No. I-08-089.) This is also consistent with our
advice in Mikesell Advice Letter, No. A-13-070 [a payment for an Alliance for Justice fellow to
assist a state agency in official agency business does not confer a personal benefit to any agency
official and is not a reportable gift under the Act.]

However, we determined that the payments for the CCST Fellow's placement with a
member of the Legislature did meet the definition of a "behested payment" payment and the
definition of "made at the behest of" the elected officer, as the payments were under the control,
direction, cooperation, and with the express prior consent of the Legislative member. (Garamendi
Advice Letter, supra.) Such payments must be reported within 30 days of the payment(s) equaling
or exceeding $5,000 from the same source in a calendar year. (Id.) This is consistent with past
advice involving payments to, or "pro bono" professional services for, a government agency that
are made at the behest of an elected officer. (See e.g., Dronenburg Advice Letter, I-13-123, [legal
services provided for a governmental purpose at no cost to a governmental agency at an elected
officer's behest are a reportable behested payment if the value exceeds $5,000 in a calendar year.]
and Bagneris Advice Letter, No. A-15-011 [city mayor's request for funds payable to the city to
support a city parade and festival resulted in behested payments that the elected officer must
report.].)

As we advised regarding the Legislative branch CCST Fellowship Program, once CCST has
paid a Fellow placed in the Executive branch at the $5,000 threshold in any one year, the elected
officer or member of the PUC coordinating or giving prior consent to the placement pursuant to
Section 82041.3 must report the payment within 30 days. The Section 84224 reporting requirement
does not apply to other state officers.

File No. I-19-238
Page No. 5

Conflict of Interest

Additionally, we caution that any Fellow who makes or participates in making agency
decisions must be designated in the agency's conflict of interest code, file a Form 700 Statement of
Economic Interests, and report the income received from CCST. A Fellow may not make,
participating in making, or influence any agency decision that may foreseeably have a material
effect on CCST as a source of income to the Fellow, or on the Fellow's personal finances. (See
Sections 1090, 87100, 87103, 87300 and 87306; Regulation 18700(a).)

Question 2

We have determined that the payments for the Fellows are not "gifts" under the Act but fall
under the category of "beshested payments" for elected officers. The reporting requirements for
beshested payments states that the report shall contain "the name of payor." (Section 84224(a).) The
Commission has previously advised that an elected officer or PUC member must report beshested
payments made from a single source, including a single donor to a nonprofit, of $5,000 or more in a
calendar year. (See Sutton Advice Letter, No. A-05-256 and Taylor Advice Letter, 03-085.). Where
the elected officer or PUC member knows or has reason to know that the single source of the
Fellow's payment is a donor to the CCST, the beshested payment report should reflect that donor as
the single source. However, for agencies where the chief officer is an appointed officer, such as the
Natural Resources Agency in your specific example, the payments would not fall under the
definition of a beshested payment under Section 82004.5.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja
